hook: You already know how to work with discrete distributions like the binomial and Poisson, where probabilities are assigned to countable outcomes. But many real-world quantities — waiting times, measurement errors, physical lifetimes — take on values across a continuous range, and a simple list of probabilities can't capture them. How do we assign and compute probabilities when outcomes fill an entire interval?
concept: The unifying idea is that continuous probability is governed by shape: instead of summing probabilities, we integrate a density function to find the likelihood of outcomes in any range. Formally, this is the study of probability measures defined by probability density functions (PDFs) and their associated cumulative distribution functions (CDFs).

topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to model and compute probabilities for waiting times and lifetimes using the exponential distribution's memoryless property and its PDF $f(x) = \lambda e^{-\lambda x}$.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Probability Density Functions and CDFs via Integration
topic_roadmap.description: Master the fundamental technique of using integration to move between a PDF and its CDF, and to compute probabilities, means, and variances for any continuous distribution.

Apply the Gamma and Beta distributions to model skewed positive-valued quantities and probabilities constrained to $[0,1]$, building fluency with two of the most versatile continuous families in statistics.

You will learn how the chi-squared distribution arises as a sum of squared standard normal variables and why it is indispensable for inference procedures like goodness-of-fit tests and variance estimation.
